meta.title: Sequence and in vivo transcription of Lacanobia oleracea granulovirus egt

abstractHtml: We have determined the nucleotide sequence and located the major in vivo transcript termini of the Lacanobia oleracea granulovirus (LoGV) egt gene. The open reading frame encodes a 460-amino acid polypeptide having extensive sequence similarity to ten nucleopolyhedrovirus (NPV) ecdysteroid UDP-glucosyltransferase (EGT) proteins; the degree of similarity is particularly high within several previously identified EGT 'domains', and eight invariant amino acid residues are conserved. A phylogenetic tree, constructed by the neighbour joining method, showed LoGV EGT to be the most highly diverged of the eleven baculovirus sequences compared. Database searching revealed that part of a published DNA sequence from Cryptophlebia leucotreta granulovirus appears to encode the N-terminal region of EGT, and the relative genomic locations of the egt and granulin genes in that virus were compared with their positions in LoGV. In infected L. oleracea larvae, egt is transcribed predominantly as a 1.6 kb mRNA. Primer extension analysis suggested that the major egt 5' transcription terminus is located within a baculovirus late gene promoter motif (GTAAG), in contrast to the early gene promoter contexts determined by others for three NPV egt mRNA 5' ends. An early transcriptional start site is also used in LoGV egt expression, but at a much lower level. The 3' terminus of egt mRNA was identified by sequencing DNA fragments generated by rapid amplification of cDNA ends, and is located 58-62 nucleotides beyond the translation stop codon.
summaryHtml:
Smith and Goodale characterized the egt gene of Lacanobia oleracea granulovirus (LoGV), a virus that infects tomato moth larvae. They determined the nucleotide sequence and transcription pattern of this gene, which encodes a 460-amino acid ecdysteroid UDP-glucosyltransferase (EGT) protein. The EGT protein is an enzyme that interferes with host larval development by preventing molting. The researchers found that the LoGV egt gene produces a predominantly 1.6 kb mRNA transcript in infected larvae. Using primer extension analysis, they identified that the major transcription start site is located within a late promoter motif (GTAAG), contrasting with NPV egt genes that initiate from early promoter sequences. A minor transcription start site also exists at lower levels, suggesting sequential gene expression. The 3' end of the mRNA was located 58-62 nucleotides downstream of the stop codon using rapid amplification of cDNA ends (RACE). Phylogenetic comparison showed LoGV EGT to be the most diverged among eleven baculovirus EGT sequences examined, though it retained eight invariant amino acid residues found in all EGT proteins.

Key findings

  • LoGV egt encodes a 460-amino acid EGT protein with sequence similarity to nucleopolyhedrovirus (NPV) EGT proteins, including eight invariant residues
  • The primary egt transcript in infected larvae is 1.6 kb mRNA, initiated mainly from a late promoter motif (GTAAG) rather than early promoters like NPV egt genes
  • LoGV EGT is the most phylogenetically diverged EGT among eleven compared baculovirus sequences, though sharing conserved structural domains
  • Transcription termination occurs 58-62 nucleotides beyond the stop codon, with evidence of alternative transcription start sites at early and late infection stages
